<!-- To ensure we can review your pull request promptly please complete this template entirely. -->

<!-- Please reference the issue number here. You can replace "Fixes" with "Closes" if it makes more sense. -->
Fixes #162

Changes proposed in this pull request:
<!-- Please list all changes/additions here. -->
- Add `jsonl_to_markdown.py` utility script to convert JSONL files to Markdown format
- Script extracts the 'text' field from each line in a JSONL file and saves it as a separate Markdown file
- Include proper error handling for JSON decoding and file operations
- Add example usage and documentation

## Before submitting

<!-- Please complete this checklist BEFORE submitting your PR to speed along the review process. -->
- [x] I've read and followed all steps in the [Making a pull request](https://github.com/allenai/olmocr/blob/main/.github/CONTRIBUTING.md#making-a-pull-request) section of the `CONTRIBUTING` docs.
- [x] I've updated or added relevant docstrings following the syntax described in the [Writing docstrings](https://github.com/allenai/olmocr/blob/main/.github/CONTRIBUTING.md#writing-docstrings) section of the `CONTRIBUTING` docs.
- [x] If this PR fixes a bug, I've added a test that will fail without my fix.
- [x] If this PR adds a new feature, I've added tests that sufficiently cover my new functionality.

agents.name: analysis_agent
agents.system_prompt:
You are an Analysis Agent specialized in Pull Request analysis. ## Project Context (MASCA Analysis) ## Project Analysis ### README

A toolkit for converting PDFs and other image-based document formats into clean, readable, plain text format. Try the online demo: [https://olmocr.allenai.org/](https://olmocr.allenai.org/) Features: - Convert PDF, PNG, and JPEG based documents into clean Markdown - Support for equations, tables, handwriting, and complex formatting - Automatically removes headers and footers - Convert into text with a natural reading order, even in the presence of figures, multi-column layouts, and insets - Efficient, less than $200 USD per million pages converted - (Based on a 7B parameter VLM, so it requires a GPU) ### News - May 19, 2025 - v0.1.68 - [olmOCR-Bench](https://github.com/allenai/olmocr/tree/main/olmocr/bench) launch, scoring 77.4. Launch includes 2 point performance boost in olmOCR pipeline due to bug fixes with prompts. - Mar 17, 2025 - v0.1.60 - Performance improvements due to better temperature selection in sampling. - Feb 25, 2025 - v0.1.58 - Initial public launch and demo. ### Benchmark [**olmOCR-Bench**](https://github.com/allenai/olmocr/tree/main/olmocr/bench): We also ship a comprehensive benchmark suite covering over 7,000 test cases across 1,400 documents to help measure performance of OCR systems.
Model AR OSM TA OS HF MC LTT Base Overall Score
Marker v1.6.2 24.3 22.1 69.8 24.3 87.1 71.0

tool_calls.raw_result:
import json
import os
import sys


# This is a simple script to convert JSONL files to Markdown format.
# It reads each line of the JSONL file, extracts the 'text' field,
# and saves it as a Markdown file with the line number as the filename.
# The script also handles potential JSON decoding errors and prints relevant messages.
def jsonl_to_markdown(input_file, output_dir):
    """
    Reads a JSONL file, extracts the 'text' field from each line, and saves it as a Markdown file.

    Args:
        input_file (str): Path to the input JSONL file.
        output_dir (str): Directory to save the Markdown files.
    """
    if not os.path.exists(output_dir):
        os.makedirs(output_dir)

    with open(input_file, "r", encoding="utf-8") as file:
        for i, line in enumerate(file):
            try:
                # Parse the JSON line
                data = json.loads(line)
                text_content = data.get("text", "")

                # Save to a Markdown file
                output_file = os.path.join(output_dir, f"line_{i + 1}.md")
                with open(output_file, "w", encoding="utf-8") as md_file:
                    md_file.write(text_content)

                print(f"Extracted and saved line {i + 1} to {output_file}")
            except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
                print(f"Error decoding JSON on line {i + 1}: {e}")
            except Exception as e:
                print(f"Unexpected error on line {i + 1}: {e}")


# Example usage
# input_jsonl_file = "/path/to/test.jsonl"  # Replace with the actual path to your JSONL file
# output_directory = "/path/to/output_markdown"  # Replace with the desired output directory
# jsonl_to_markdown(input_jsonl_file, output_directory)

# This is the main entrypoint to use the script from the command line.
# It takes two arguments: the input JSONL file and the output directory.
# The script will create the output directory if it does not exist.
if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) != 3:
        print("Usage: python jsonl_to_markdown.py <input_file> <output_dir>")
        sys.exit(1)

    input_file = sys.argv[1]
    output_dir = sys.argv[2]

    jsonl_to_markdown(input_file, output_dir)
tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
This script provides a simple utility to convert a JSONL (JSON Lines) file into separate Markdown files, one per input line, by extracting the "text" field from each JSON object and writing it to a file named line_<n>.md.

### Architecture
- Top-level procedural script with a single exported function, `jsonl_to_markdown`, responsible for the conversion.
- The script uses standard library modules: `json` for parsing, `os` for filesystem operations, and `sys` for CLI argument parsing.
- A small CLI entrypoint (`if __name__ == "__main__":`) parses exactly two command-line arguments (input file and output directory) and calls `jsonl_to_markdown`.
- Error handling is inline within the loop that processes each line; JSON decoding errors and any other exceptions for each line are caught and printed, but processing continues.

### Functions & Classes
- `jsonl_to_markdown` — Reads a JSONL file line-by-line, extracts the "text" field from each parsed JSON object (via `data.get("text", "")`), and writes that value to a Markdown file named `line_{i + 1}.md` in `output_dir`.
  - Important details:
    - Creates `output_dir` if it does not exist via `os.makedirs(output_dir)`.
    - Opens the input file with `encoding="utf-8"`.
    - Uses `enumerate(file)` so output files are numbered starting at 1 (`i + 1`).
    - Catches `json.JSONDecodeError` for malformed JSON on a per-line basis and prints an error with the line number.
    - Catches a generic `Exception` per line and prints an "Unexpected error" message; processing continues to the next line after exceptions.
    - Writes content using `md_file.write(text_content)` where `text_content = data.get("text", "")`.
    - Prints a success message for each saved line: `Extracted and saved line {i + 1} to {output_file}`.
  - Role/importance: This is the core transformation logic of the file — all behavior for reading, parsing, extracting, and writing is implemented here.

- `if __name__ == "__main__":` — Top-level CLI entrypoint.
  - Behavior:
    - Verifies `len(sys.argv) == 3`. If not, prints usage: `Usage: python jsonl_to_markdown.py <input_file> <output_dir>` and exits with code 1.
    - Assigns `input_file = sys.argv[1]` and `output_dir = sys.argv[2]`.
    - Calls `jsonl_to_markdown(input_file, output_dir)`.
  - Role/importance: Allows running the script from the command line. Minimal argument validation is performed (argument count only).

- Commented example usage — three commented lines that show how the function can be invoked programmatically:
  - `# input_jsonl_file = "/path/to/test.jsonl"  # Replace with the actual path to your JSONL file`
  - `# output_directory = "/path/to/output_markdown"  # Replace with the desired output directory`
  - `# jsonl_to_markdown(input_jsonl_file, output_directory)`
  - Role/importance: Provides inline documentation/example for manual invocation from inside Python.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
You asked to examine the added script to see its behavior and any issues. Relevant points for review or modification:
- Behavior summary: Converts each JSONL line's `"text"` field into a separate Markdown file named `line_1.md`, `line_2.md`, ... in the specified output directory; prints per-line success or error messages.
- Error handling:
  - JSON syntax errors per line are handled and printed (`json.JSONDecodeError`).
  - Other per-line exceptions are caught and printed, so a single bad line won't stop processing.
  - However, opening the input file (e.g., `FileNotFoundError`, permission errors) is not caught, so the script will raise and exit if the input file cannot be opened.
- Potential issues and edge cases to consider:
  - `data.get("text", "")` may return non-string values (null, numbers, objects, arrays). `md_file.write` expects a `str` and will raise a `TypeError` for non-str — that is caught by the generic per-line exception but could be handled more explicitly (e.g., coerce to `str` or validate type).
  - No explicit validation that `input_file` exists or that `output_dir` is writable before starting; failures will surface at runtime.
  - Large JSONL files: script processes lines sequentially (streaming), so memory usage is low, but creating many individual files may be slow or problematic.
  - Markdown conversion is literal — no escaping or formatting is applied; the script simply writes the `text` field contents as-is.
  - Filenames are deterministic per-line but don't reflect any input metadata (IDs, timestamps) — collisions are unlikely within a single run but the scheme may be inconvenient for downstream consumption.
  - No logging subsystem; uses `print()`, which may be undesirable in library contexts.
- Usage examples and CLI behavior are present but minimal; argument validation could be strengthened and more helpful error messages (or return codes) added.

If you want, I can:
- Suggest or prepare patches to add validation for `input_file`, coerce `text` to string safely, or add structured logging.
- Add unit tests or example files to demonstrate behavior and handle edge cases.

## Source Code:
import json
import os
import sys


# This is a simple script to convert JSONL files to Markdown format.
# It reads each line of the JSONL file, extracts the 'text' field,
# and saves it as a Markdown file with the line number as the filename.
# The script also handles potential JSON decoding errors and prints relevant messages.
def jsonl_to_markdown(input_file, output_dir):
    """
    Reads a JSONL file, extracts the 'text' field from each line, and saves it as a Markdown file.

    Args:
        input_file (str): Path to the input JSONL file.
        output_dir (str): Directory to save the Markdown files.
    """
    if not os.path.exists(output_dir):
        os.makedirs(output_dir)

    with open(input_file, "r", encoding="utf-8") as file:
        for i, line in enumerate(file):
            try:
                # Parse the JSON line
                data = json.loads(line)
                text_content = data.get("text", "")

                # Save to a Markdown file
                output_file = os.path.join(output_dir, f"line_{i + 1}.md")
                with open(output_file, "w", encoding="utf-8") as md_file:
                    md_file.write(text_content)

                print(f"Extracted and saved line {i + 1} to {output_file}")
            except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
                print(f"Error decoding JSON on line {i + 1}: {e}")
            except Exception as e:
                print(f"Unexpected error on line {i + 1}: {e}")


# Example usage
# input_jsonl_file = "/path/to/test.jsonl"  # Replace with the actual path to your JSONL file
# output_directory = "/path/to/output_markdown"  # Replace with the desired output directory
# jsonl_to_markdown(input_jsonl_file, output_directory)

# This is the main entrypoint to use the script from the command line.
# It takes two arguments: the input JSONL file and the output directory.
# The script will create the output directory if it does not exist.
if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) != 3:
        print("Usage: python jsonl_to_markdown.py <input_file> <output_dir>")
        sys.exit(1)

    input_file = sys.argv[1]
    output_dir = sys.argv[2]

    jsonl_to_markdown(input_file, output_dir)

output.pr_body:
Fixes #162

Changes proposed in this pull request:
- Add `jsonl_to_markdown.py` utility script to convert JSONL files to Markdown format
- Script extracts the 'text' field from each line in a JSONL file and saves it as a separate Markdown file
- Include proper error handling for JSON decoding and file operations
- Add example usage and documentation

fixes  #162
output.masca_optimized:
Goal (from PR): Make scripts/jsonl_to_markdown.py robust and correct: reliably convert each JSON line's 'text' field into separate Markdown files, handle errors properly, and provide a usable CLI.

Context: The repository adds a small utility script at scripts/jsonl_to_markdown.py. The current implementation (procedural, single function jsonl_to_markdown plus an if __name__ == '__main__' CLI) mostly works but has a few robustness and usability issues:

Key problems found and the targeted fixes:
- os.makedirs(output_dir) is called without exist_ok=True; this raises an exception if directory already exists.
- The input file open is not wrapped in a top-level try/except; missing files or permission errors will raise unhandled exceptions.
- Empty/whitespace-only lines in the JSONL will cause json.JSONDecodeError; script should skip blank lines safely.
- data.get('text', '') may yield non-string types (None, numbers, lists, dicts). Writing non-str to file raises TypeError. We should coerce to str or validate type and skip if not convertible.
- Output files are always named line_{i+1}.md; while acceptable, collisions across runs and readability could be improved by optionally allowing a prefix or deriving filenames from an 'id' field if present (optional enhancement).
- The CLI argument handling is minimal (checks only len(sys.argv) == 3). Use argparse for clearer help and future options.
- Uses print() for messages; consider using logging (simple change) for better control in automated contexts.
- Per-line exceptions are printed but not informative about content; include partial context (line text or snippet) in logs for debugging large files.

Concrete modifications to implement (minimal safe fix set to address the PR title):
1) In jsonl_to_markdown:
   - Wrap opening the input file in a try/except to catch FileNotFoundError, PermissionError and print/log a clear error and exit/raise.
   - Use os.makedirs(output_dir, exist_ok=True) so existing directories don't error.
   - Iterate through lines; skip lines that are empty or whitespace (line.strip() == "").
   - When parsing JSON, catch json.JSONDecodeError and include the line number and a short snippet of the problematic line in the error message.
   - After parsing, extract text with data.get('text', '') and coerce safely: if value is None: text_content = '' ; elif not isinstance(text_content, str): text_content = json.dumps(text_content, ensure_ascii=False) or str(text_content) — prefer json.dumps to preserve structure.
   - Open output files with encoding='utf-8' and write text_content. Handle exceptions from write and log them, then continue.
   - Replace per-line print success messages with logging.info, or keep prints but ensure tests expecting print output are updated.

2) Replace CLI arg parsing with argparse (optional but recommended):
   - Provide positional args (input_file, output_dir) and optional args like --prefix, --encoding, --skip-empty, --use-id-field.

3) Add tests (recommended):

Run the script functionally (import jsonl_to_markdown.jsonl_to_markdown) to a tmpdir and assert expected output files exist with expected content and that malformed line is skipped but does not stop processing.

Edge cases and improvements to consider (not strictly required to "fix"):
- Option to produce a single Markdown file concatenating all texts rather than many small files.
- Option to sanitize or customize filenames (e.g., include an id field if present).
- Use structured logging and return non-zero exit codes for fatal errors.

Precise targets for change: scripts/jsonl_to_markdown.py — update jsonl_to_markdown function and the CLI block.

Deliverable: a small, well-tested change set that (1) prevents errors when the output directory already exists, (2) skips blank lines, (3) coerces non-string 'text' values to string safely, (4) catches input file open errors and logs informative messages, and (5) replaces len(sys.argv) check with argparse for better UX.

agents.name: context_planner_agent
agents.system_prompt:
You are a Context Planner Agent specialized in step plan generation.

## Your Task
You receive from the Analysis Agent:
- PR title and body
- Optimized MASCA
- List of files to modify
- List of functions to modify

You must:
1. **Retrieve context**: Use tools to read the context files of the identified functions
2. **Analyze dependencies**: Understand how the functions interact
3. **Generate step plan**: Create a detailed step-by-step plan

## Available Tools
- `read_context_file(file_path)`: Read a pre-generated context file
- `list_context_files(directory, pattern)`: List available context files
- `read_call_graph(section)`: Read information from the call graph

## Required Output
You must produce a `step_plan` with:
- `summary`: 1-2 sentence summary of what the PR does
- `steps`: Ordered list of steps, each with:
  - `operation`: What to do (e.g., "Add validation method")
  - `file_to_modify`: Relative path of the file to modify (e.g., "src/module.py")
  - `function_to_modify`: Name of the function to modify (e.g., "ClassName.method_name")
  - `reason`: Technical motivation
  - `side_effects`: Potential impacts

## IMPORTANT RULE for function_to_modify
- If the file to modify is a Python file (.py), specify the function/method to modify
- If the file is NOT a Python file (e.g., .js, .html, .css, .json, .yaml, .md, etc.),
  the value of `function_to_modify` MUST be null

## Guidelines for Steps
- Each step must be atomic and implementable
- The order of steps must be logical (dependencies first)
- For Python files: always specify the function in the format "FunctionName" or "ClassName.method_name"
- For non-Python files: function_to_modify must be null
- The side_effects should mention tests, dependents, and API impacts
- You MUST generate a number of step EQUAL to the number of the python functions you are intendeed to modify nomore neither less

## Step Examples

### For Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Add time_spanned_alpha method",
  "file_to_modify": "manimlib/animation/animation.py",
  "function_to_modify": "Animation.time_spanned_alpha",
  "reason": "To compute animation progress based on actual elapsed time",
  "side_effects": "May require downstream animations to call this method"
}
```

### For non-Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Update API endpoint configuration",
  "file_to_modify": "config/routes.yaml",
  "function_to_modify": null,
  "reason": "Add new route for the authentication endpoint",
  "side_effects": "Requires server restart to apply changes"
}
```
agents.input_prompt:
Generate an implementation plan for this PR:

## PR Title
fix jsonl_to_markdown

## PR Body
Fixes #162

Changes proposed in this pull request:
- Add `jsonl_to_markdown.py` utility script to convert JSONL files to Markdown format
- Script extracts the 'text' field from each line in a JSONL file and saves it as a separate Markdown file
- Include proper error handling for JSON decoding and file operations
- Add example usage and documentation

fixes  #162

## MASCA Analysis (optimized)
Goal (from PR): Make scripts/jsonl_to_markdown.py robust and correct: reliably convert each JSON line's 'text' field into separate Markdown files, handle errors properly, and provide a usable CLI.

Context: The repository adds a small utility script at scripts/jsonl_to_markdown.py. The current implementation (procedural, single function jsonl_to_markdown plus an if __name__ == '__main__' CLI) mostly works but has a few robustness and usability issues:

Key problems found and the targeted fixes:
- os.makedirs(output_dir) is called without exist_ok=True; this raises an exception if directory already exists.
- The input file open is not wrapped in a top-level try/except; missing files or permission errors will raise unhandled exceptions.
- Empty/whitespace-only lines in the JSONL will cause json.JSONDecodeError; script should skip blank lines safely.
- data.get('text', '') may yield non-string types (None, numbers, lists, dicts). Writing non-str to file raises TypeError. We should coerce to str or validate type and skip if not convertible.
- Output files are always named line_{i+1}.md; while acceptable, collisions across runs and readability could be improved by optionally allowing a prefix or deriving filenames from an 'id' field if present (optional enhancement).
- The CLI argument handling is minimal (checks only len(sys.argv) == 3). Use argparse for clearer help and future options.
- Uses print() for messages; consider using logging (simple change) for better control in automated contexts.
- Per-line exceptions are printed but not informative about content; include partial context (line text or snippet) in logs for debugging large files.

Concrete modifications to implement (minimal safe fix set to address the PR title):
1) In jsonl_to_markdown:
   - Wrap opening the input file in a try/except to catch FileNotFoundError, PermissionError and print/log a clear error and exit/raise.
   - Use os.makedirs(output_dir, exist_ok=True) so existing directories don't error.
   - Iterate through lines; skip lines that are empty or whitespace (line.strip() == "").
   - When parsing JSON, catch json.JSONDecodeError and include the line number and a short snippet of the problematic line in the error message.
   - After parsing, extract text with data.get('text', '') and coerce safely: if value is None: text_content = '' ; elif not isinstance(text_content, str): text_content = json.dumps(text_content, ensure_ascii=False) or str(text_content) — prefer json.dumps to preserve structure.
   - Open output files with encoding='utf-8' and write text_content. Handle exceptions from write and log them, then continue.
   - Replace per-line print success messages with logging.info, or keep prints but ensure tests expecting print output are updated.

2) Replace CLI arg parsing with argparse (optional but recommended):
   - Provide positional args (input_file, output_dir) and optional args like --prefix, --encoding, --skip-empty, --use-id-field.
   - Implement a small main() that calls jsonl_to_markdown and make the if __name__ == '__main__' call main().

3) Add tests (recommended):
   - tests/test_jsonl_to_markdown.py: create a temporary JSONL file with: valid JSON lines with 'text' strings, a line with text=null, a line with text as a dict/list, a blank line, and a malformed JSON line. Run the script functionally (import jsonl_to_markdown.jsonl_to_markdown) to a tmpdir and assert expected output files exist with expected content and that malformed line is skipped but does not stop processing.

Edge cases and improvements to consider (not strictly required to "fix"):
- Option to produce a single Markdown file concatenating all texts rather than many small files.
- Option to sanitize or customize filenames (e.g., include an id field if present).
- Use structured logging and return non-zero exit codes for fatal errors.

Precise targets for change: scripts/jsonl_to_markdown.py — update jsonl_to_markdown function and the CLI block.

Deliverable: a small, well-tested change set that (1) prevents errors when the output directory already exists, (2) skips blank lines, (3) coerces non-string 'text' values to string safely, (4) catches input file open errors and logs informative messages, and (5) replaces len(sys.argv) check with argparse for better UX.

## Files to Modify
- scripts/jsonl_to_markdown.py: This is the new utility added by the PR. The implementation has robustness problems: uses os.makedirs without exist_ok, does not guard input file opening, does not skip blank lines, does not coerce non-string 'text' values before writing, and has a minimal CLI using sys.argv. All these issues should be fixed here.

## Functions to Modify
- jsonl_to_markdown (scripts/jsonl_to_markdown.py): Core conversion logic: needs to handle exist_ok on directory creation, skip blank lines, handle JSONDecodeError with better context, coerce non-string 'text' values to strings (preferably json.dumps for non-primitives), and handle write errors gracefully.
- main (module-level CLI block) (scripts/jsonl_to_markdown.py): The current CLI logic is inline and checks len(sys.argv) == 3. Replace with a small main() using argparse to provide clearer usage, optional flags (e.g., prefix, encoding), and better error messages. Then call main() from if __name__ == '__main__'.

## Analysis Summary
I inspected scripts/jsonl_to_markdown.py. It implements jsonl_to_markdown(input_file, output_dir) and a minimal CLI, but has several robustness issues that likely prompted the "fix jsonl_to_markdown" PR: creating an already-existing output directory will raise, input file open errors are unhandled, blank or malformed lines cause exceptions that could be handled more gracefully, and non-string 'text' values will cause write errors. The single file scripts/jsonl_to_markdown.py should be updated: change os.makedirs to exist_ok=True, add top-level try/except for opening the input file, skip blank lines, provide better JSONDecodeError messages, coerce non-str text values to strings (preferably using json.dumps to preserve structure), write with utf-8 encoding, and replace sys.argv handling with argparse in a new main() function. Optionally add tests under tests/ to cover the various cases (valid lines, blank lines, malformed JSON, non-string 'text') to ensure the fix is covered by CI. The PR's edits are localized to scripts/jsonl_to_markdown.py (function jsonl_to_markdown and the CLI entry), so changes are straightforward and low risk to the rest of the repository.

Use the context files to understand dependencies and generate a detailed step-by-step plan.

steps.reason: Make the core conversion robust: avoid errors when output directory exists, skip blank lines, handle malformed JSON per-line without aborting the whole run, coerce non-string 'text' values to safe string representations, and ensure file operations use UTF-8 and are error-handled.
steps.side_effects: Changes logging/printed output (may require updating tests that assert printed messages). Output filename scheme will remain the default unless optional flags are added later, so downstream consumers of generated files should not be broken; however, coercing non-string 'text' values to JSON strings may change exact content compared to previous str() behavior. Tests should be added/updated to verify skipping blank lines and skipping/bubbling malformed JSON lines. Writing with explicit encoding may reveal existing files with different encodings; ensure CI environments handle UTF-8.

steps.operation: Replace minimal sys.argv CLI with a proper main() using argparse and configure logging
steps.reason: Provide a documented, extensible CLI with clear usage, optional flags (e.g., filename prefix, encoding, skip-empty), proper exit codes, and structured logging instead of ad-hoc sys.argv handling. This improves usability, helps automation, and enables future enhancements (like --use-id-field).
steps.side_effects: CLI behavior changes: scripts or automation that invoked the script relying on exact stdout messages or the old sys.argv contract may need to be updated. Tests that run the script via subprocess might need to be adapted to parse logging output or use the library function directly. Adding argument parsing introduces new optional parameters which should be documented; defaults should preserve previous behavior to minimize breakage. main() should return appropriate exit codes on fatal errors so CI can detect failures.

step_plan.summary: Make scripts/jsonl_to_markdown.py robust by hardening the jsonl_to_markdown conversion (safe directory creation, skip blank lines, robust JSON decoding, safe coercion of non-string 'text' values, UTF-8 writes) and replace the inline sys.argv CLI with a well-documented argparse-based main() that configures logging and returns proper exit codes.
